title: Wild mushroom and chestnut soup
subtitle: Deep, rich, and unmistakably autumnal
excerpt: A mix of fresh wild mushrooms and vacuum-packed chestnuts, cooked with shallots, thyme, and Madeira, then blended until thick and smooth. One of the most intensely flavoured autumn soups there is.
sourceNotes: Wild mushroom and chestnut soup draws on French and British autumn cooking traditions where mushrooms and chestnuts are treated as serious flavouring ingredients. No single canonical source.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to two days ahead without cream. Add cream when reheating over a gentle heat.

glossaryTerms.definition: Simmering a liquid in an open pan to evaporate water and concentrate the flavour. When wine or Madeira is reduced before stock is added, it loses its raw alcohol edge and leaves only the deep, sweet flavour behind.

content.text: Wild mushrooms and chestnuts have an affinity that makes this soup more than the sum of its parts. The mushrooms provide earthiness and depth; the chestnuts add a starchy sweetness and a creaminess that cream alone cannot replicate. Madeira, added after the mushrooms are cooked and

before the stock goes in, gives the soup a warm, rounded quality that sets it apart from a standard mushroom soup. Use whatever wild or mixed mushrooms are available ,  chanterelles, girolles, oyster, or a supermarket mixed pack all work.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the {{shallot}} shallots with a pinch of salt and cook for 6 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and {{thyme}} of thyme and cook for 1 minute.

Add the {{mushrooms-chestnut}} of sliced mushrooms. Turn the heat up to medium and cook for 10 to 12 minutes, stirring occasionally, until they have given off their liquid and the pan is nearly dry. This is where the flavour builds.
Add the {{chestnuts}} of chopped chestnuts and stir for 1 minute. Pour in {{fortified-madeira}} of Madeira and let it reduce by half ,  about 2 minutes.
Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of hot stock. Simmer for 10 minutes.
Remove the thyme stalks. Blend in a jug blender until very smooth. Stir in {{double-cream}} of cream. Taste and season.
Ladle into bowls. Add a swirl of cream and a few thyme leaves if desired.

items.symptom: Soup tastes flat despite the ingredients
items.cause: The mushrooms were not cooked until properly dry and beginning to brown.
items.fix: The step where the mushroom liquid evaporates is critical. Do not rush it or add stock too early. The mushrooms must concentrate before anything else goes in.

items.cause: The Madeira was not reduced enough, leaving raw alcohol and over-sweet wine notes.
items.fix: Reduce the Madeira fully until less than half remains and the steam smells sweet rather than alcoholic. If too strong after blending, a squeeze of lemon juice helps.

items.cause: Chestnuts can leave a slightly grainy texture if not blended long enough.
items.fix: Blend for at least 60 to 90 seconds in a jug blender. Pass through a fine-mesh sieve for a very smooth finish.

items.cause: Chestnuts are starchy and thicken the soup considerably.
items.fix: Thin with hot stock or water a ladleful at a time after blending. The consistency should pour easily from a ladle.

content.text: Wild mushrooms and chestnuts are autumn ingredients in both France and Britain: both appear in the forest at the same time of year and have been paired in cooking for centuries. In France, the combination appears in velouté de champignons and chestnut soups (soupe aux châtaignes) of the Périgord and Ardèche regions. In Britain, the soup became a restaurant and gastropub autumn staple in the 1990s. Vacuum-packed chestnuts, available year-round, make it a practical recipe beyond the October harvest.
